On this page

LinkedQL Guides ​

This section is the practical entry point for getting LinkedQL running in a real application.

The guides are organized by runtime shape or engine:

  • PostgreSQL
  • MySQL
  • MariaDB
  • FlashQL
  • Edge
  • Integration Patterns

Choose Your Starting Shape ​

ShapeUse it whenStart here
Direct database queryingyour app can connect to the database directlyPostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB
Cross-runtime queryingyour app runs in the browser, edge, or another boundary and needs remote DB accessEdge
Local-first queryingyou want the database inside the app, with optional upstream syncFlashQL

Installation ​

Install the package from npm:

bash
npm install @linked-db/linked-ql

The package exports the client entry points.

ClientImport PathGuide
PGClient@linked-db/linked-ql/postgresPostgreSQL
MySQLClient@linked-db/linked-ql/mysqlMySQL
MariaDBClient@linked-db/linked-ql/mariadbMariaDB
FlashQL@linked-db/linked-ql/flashqlFlashQL
EdgeClient@linked-db/linked-ql/edgeEdge
EdgeWorker@linked-db/linked-ql/edge-workerEdge

All clients share the same top-level contract:

  • db.query()
  • db.stream()
  • db.transaction()
  • db.wal.subscribe()

Enabling Realtime ​

LinkedQL's realtime capabilities depend on the underlying runtime:

  • PostgreSQL requires logical replication
  • MySQL and MariaDB are designed around binlog-based support, but live queries and changefeeds are not yet available there
  • FlashQL supports realtime out of the box
  • Edge transports realtime capabilities from the upstream runtime behind EdgeWorker

For the details, see:

  • PostgreSQL realtime setup
  • MySQL realtime notes
  • MariaDB realtime notes
  • FlashQL realtime notes
  • Edge realtime notes

Runtime Tuning Flags ​

Some options tune execution behavior across clients.

nonDDLMode ​

nonDDLMode tells LinkedQL that the application is running against a stable schema and is not expected to execute schema-changing statements while the process is alive.

js
const db = new PGClient({
  host: 'localhost',
  database: 'myapp',
  nonDDLMode: true,
});

When enabled, LinkedQL can avoid repeatedly re-checking schema state around DDL boundaries and can safely keep schema inference work cached in memory for the life of the process.

This is mainly a production optimization:

  • use it when your app treats schema as fixed during normal runtime
  • leave it off when migrations or ad-hoc DDL may occur while the app is running

What To Read Next ​

  • If you want the method-by-method contract, go to API
  • If you want SQL-level extensions, go to Language
  • If you want live/reactive behavior, go to Realtime
  • If you want deployment composition guidance, go to Integration Patterns
